title: Through the Fire
premise:
Using this RPG lore PDF I've given you, construct a sexy, erotic CHYOA with a realistic edge to it. Before we start, we're going to construct our protagonist. Everything will be written in 2nd person, and decisions will be frequent. At the end of every story section, give 4 different paths/choices, but be flexible to specific custom inputs. Focus on crafting an exciting overarching narrative that bends to the user's desires.

Sex scenes should not be a one-and-done event, but instead longer events that span several choices, to immerse the reader and give them a sense of choice. The same for fighting and other appropriate story scenes. Be vivid with the sex.

Our protagonist is a young man named Joseph Bergstrom. He grew up in the Sprague River Valley, living at the edge of the Nation Forest in a log cabin with his father. He has been working a man-sized job cutting firewood with his father since he was 8, and is a seasoned woodsman. His skills are eclectic and for the "real world" fairly useless, but useful in the alternate world he has found himself in. He can ride a horse, build a log house, make his own bow and arrows, skin an animal, cure hides, build traps, and is well-read and self-educated to a high degree, even if he still keeps his rural accent and way of talking. Bergstrom is of primarily Swedish and Cherokee heritage, and he is mildly superstitious (having been raised on the Norse and Cherokee legends and myths), but is a Christian. Bergstrom is even in his native Oregon a very tall man, standing 6'3", but in the alternate Oregon with its constant war and nutritional deficits, he would be extremely tall. While tall, his is not built large in many other ways, and is lanky, but extremely strong. Even his feet are a little small, at only real-world size 10.5, though the old wives tale about foot size and penis size being related is not too true with him. In fact his cock is 8.5 inches long and thick. He has dark hair, intense blue eyes, and a thick beard.

Bergstrom is plopped into this new world as he is fighting a wildfire alongside his neighbors, where he finds himself alone and cut off from everything he knows. His first encounter is with what he would call "goblins". Make sure that Bergstrom has no knowledge of the lore or the names of anything until it is revealed to him naturally in the narrative, most likely in dialogue.
worldInfo:
=== Oregon RPG.pdf ===
Here is the comprehensive World Bible for   Cascadia   (also known to the Elves as   Alsea ).  CASCADIA: THE GREEN DOMINION  A Low-Fantasy "Isekai" Setting  I. THE PREMISE  The world is not a distant planet or a video game simulation. It is a secondary reality overlaying the  geography of the Pacific Northwest. It is a world where the "Great Filter" of civilization broke  differently. The geography is familiar to a modern Oregonian, but it is wilder, hostile, and ancient.  The Protagonist:   A modern Oregonian whom the user can customize. He arrives via a "Burnover"  event. To the locals, he is a man out of time, speaking the tongue of ancient kings and wielding  "magic" (modern knowledge/technology).  II. THE GEOGRAPHY  The map is identical to Oregon, but untamed. The climate is intensified, and the distances feel vast  without roads.  The West: The Wet Valley & The Coast  • The Drowned Coast (Oregon Coast):   A fog-choked rainforest of "God-Tether" trees  (monstrous Redwoods/Firs). The ocean is terrifying, plagued by leviathans. A supernatural fog  called   "The Weeper"   dampens sound and magic here.  • The Wet Valley (Willamette Valley):   The heart of human civilization. A massive floodplain of  oak savannas and wetlands.  • The Great Artery:   The Willamette River. The primary trade route.  • The Rogue-Rift (Southern Oregon):   Isolated, mountainous terrain. Home to the "Sun-Cult"  Wyllem who are culturally distinct from the Valley.  The Divider: The Wall (The Cascades)  Known as   The Wall   or   The Cold Spine . A jagged, icy barrier believed to have been raised by ancient  gods (The Architects) to shield the West from the horrors of the East.  • The Three Peaks:  • Wy’east (Mt. Hood):   The Smoking Giant. Active and feared.  • Kla-Mutt (Mt. Jefferson):   The Stone Sentinel.  • The Watchers (The Three Sisters):   Rarely seen through the snow, guarding the high  passes.

The East: The Scarp & The Burned Lands  • The Scarp Lands (Central/Southern Oregon):   A high-desert frontier. Arid, rocky, and  desperate. This is the "Bleeding Border" where humanity holds the line against the Malborn.  • Kla-Mutt’s Teeth (Klamath Falls/Chiloquin):   The largest fortress-settlement of the East.  Heavily militarized.  •   Martsford (Sprague River) and Mart’s Valley (Sprague River Valley): A large valley  east of The Wall, that is surprisingly verdant, though it is still the high desert. It  is only accessible through a handful of routes.  •   The Saddle Mountain (Saddle Mountain): One of the largest mountains  surrounding Mart’s Valley, on the southwestern side.  •   Flattop Mountain (Cooks Mountain): Another on of the largest  mountains surrounding Mart’s Valley, on the northern side.  •   Tall Mountain (Bly Mountain): The tallest mountain around Mart’s Valley,  reaching 9,000 feet. It is on the southeastern side.  • The Salt-Hearth (Burns):   A grim fort protecting vital salt deposits.  • The Lake Watch (Lakeview):   The furthest outpost, ruled by paranoid lords, often used  as bait for raids.  • The Burned Lands (High Desert/Basin):   A volcanic wasteland of obsidian and sulfur.  • The Black Scar:   The point of origin for the Malborn.  • The Blue-Woven Hills (Northeast):   The Wallowa/Blue Mountains area.  • Joseph’s Hearth (Enterprise/Joseph):   The "breadbasket" of the East, isolated and  paranoid about outsiders.  III. THE RACES (THE HOMINIDS)  In Cascadia, races are distinct species of the genus   Homo , evolved or warped by ancient magic.  1. The Wyllem (Humans)  • Biology:   Baseline humans.  • Culture:   Early Medieval/Iron Age.  • The West (Valley Wyllem):   Feudal, agrarian, wealthy. They value land, lineage, and stability.  They view the East as a cursed graveyard.  • The East (Scarp Wyllem):   Survivalist, communal, militaristic. They view the West as soft and  ungrateful. They live in "Hearth-Guard" units (fortified family compounds).  2. The Viridians (Elves)  • Biology:   400–500 year lifespan. Taller, lanky, large dark eyes. Can interbreed with humans  (offspring are called   Saplings ).  • Culture:   Isolationist arbor-crafters. They do not build; they "sing" trees into shapes. They live  in the   Drowned Coast   and the high passes of   The Wall .

• Marriage:   Serial Monogamy.   They pair for a "Bloom Period" (50-100 years) to raise one child,  then ritually separate ("The Un-Knotting") to find new partners. They find human  urgency/polygamy baffling and repulsive.  3. The Malborn (Orcs)  • Origin:   Created by a magical cataclysm in the Black Scar eons ago. They are a biological curse  given flesh.  • Biology:   Exclusively Male.   They are parasitic breeders. They require a female host (Human or  Elf) to reproduce. The child is   always   a male Malborn.  • Culture:   The "Obsidian Horde." A disciplined, Roman-Legion-style military society. They do  not farm; they raid for resources and women ("Brood-Stock").  • Sub-species:   Mal-Scourge   (Goblins/Kobolds) are the "runts" or genetic mistakes, used as  fodder and scouts.  4. The Sk’et (Sasquatch)  • Biology:   7-9 foot tall, solitary hominids. Covered in fur.  • Magic:   Possess a passive   "Cloaking Knack" —a psychic field that makes the eyes of other  species slide off them. They are nearly impossible to track or photograph.  • Culture:   None. They are fiercely territorial, solitary animals. They gather only once a year for  the "Autumn Roam" to mate. They are a neutral force of nature, killing Wyllem and Malborn  alike.  IV. THE BREACHES (HISTORY)  Cascadia has been touched by our world before. These "Breaches" brought people, technology, and  culture that shaped the Wyllem civilization.  1. The Old Madness (3000+ Years Ago):   A lost civilization (perhaps future Earth or Atlantis  equivalent) that destroyed itself, creating the Burned Lands and the Malborn.  2. The Northmen’s Trail (c. 1000 AD):   Vikings who walked down the ice corridor.  • Legacy:   The "Long-Hall" architecture, the "Berserker" fighting style, and the axe as a  noble weapon.  3. The Great Anglishing (c. 1500s AD):   An Elizabethan English fleet ported into the Columbia  River.   This is the dominant cultural influence.  • Legacy:   The   Low Anglish   language (Shakespearean dialect), the concept of written law,  and noble house names (Miller, Smith, Johnson).  4. The Spanish Scar (c. 1600s AD):   A shipwreck in the south.  • Legacy:   The   Sun-God Cult   of the Rogue-Rift and the obsession with "Golden Cities."  5. The Trappers (c. 1800s AD):   French-Canadian mountain men.  • Legacy:   Advanced trapping technology and French loanwords in the deep woods.  6. The Gold Madness (c. 1850s AD):   Prospectors in the Northeast.  • Legacy:   Paranoid isolationism in Joseph’s Hearth and the distrust of digging too deep.  7. The Modern Breach (Present Day):   The Protagonist.

• Legacy:   Not yet known...  V. SOCIETY & CULTURE  Language  • Low Anglish:   Spoken by Valley Wyllem. A slurry, archaic English (mix of Elizabethan and rural  drawl).   “ Thee,” “Thou,” “Yonder.”  • Hard Common:   Spoken by Scarp Wyllem. Rougher, utilitarian, clipped.  • High English:   Spoken by the Protagonist. Sounds like the "Language of Kings" or ancient  spells to the locals.  Marriage & Polygamy  A stark divide caused by the Malborn War.  The West (Valley Wyllem):  • System:   Noble Polygyny.  • Purpose:   Status and Politics.  • Dynamic:   A sign of wealth. Only Lords can afford multiple wives. It is used to cement  alliances between families. Wives are often political assets.  The East (Scarp Wyllem):  • System:   Necessity Polygyny / The Hearth-Guard.  • Purpose:   Survival and Demographics.  • Dynamic:   Due to high male mortality rates against the Malborn, surviving warriors must  support multiple women to maintain the population.  • The "Shield-Brother" Pact:   It is culturally normal for a man to share his wives with a trusted  "Shield-Brother" or battle companion to ensure conception and shared protection.  • Agency:   Eastern women are not passive victims; they are active partners in the   Hearth-  Guard . They often prefer the security of a large, multi-adult family unit. They fight, work, and  vote on family decisions.  VI. MAGIC & LORE  The Myths  • The Ghost Roads:   The straight, unnatural lines cut through the wilderness (ruins of Interstate  5 and Hwy 97). Locals fear them, calling them "Lines of the Ancient Madness."  • The Windigo:   A real affliction. Wyllem who eat human flesh or stay too long in the deep  radiation of the Burned Lands turn into gaunt, iron-immune monsters.  • The Beaver Cult:   The Beaver is a sacred animal to the Wyllem, symbolizing industry and  hydro-engineering. Killing a beaver is bad luck; destroying a dam is a crime.

recentTurns.content:
The roar of the blaze is a living thing, a hungry god of soot and orange light tearing through the Sprague River Valley. You stand on the line, the heavy Pulaski tool in your calloused hands, lungs screaming against the thick, resinous smoke. Your father’s voice is lost to the wind, and the heat becomes an physical weight, pressing you into the scorched earth. When the wall of fire finally jumps the line, there is no scream, only a blinding white flash and a sudden, sickening silence that rings in your ears like a struck bell.

You wake up face-down in pine needles that feel like needles of ice. The air is wrong—too crisp, too heavy with the scent of damp earth and something metallic. You roll over, your 6'3" frame aching, your blue eyes squinting against a sun that sits too low and too pale in the sky. Behind you, the familiar silhouette of Saddle Mountain remains, but it looks more jagged, more feral, stripped of the logging roads you’ve known since you were eight. You are in Mart’s Valley, but the world has been twisted into something ancient and terrifying.

A sharp chittering sound breaks the silence. You freeze, your hand instinctively reaching for the Pulaski lying nearby. Emerging from a thicket of gnarled, obsidian-barked trees are three creatures that turn your stomach. They are small, barely reaching your waist, with skin the color of a fresh bruise and yellow, pupilless eyes. They wear loincloths of cured rat-skin and carry jagged blades of rusted iron. They aren't the trolls of your grandfather’s stories, but something far more visceral. They snarl, showing rows of needle-teeth, their nostrils flaring as they catch the scent of your 'High English' sweat. To them, you are a giant, a windfall of meat and strange cloth. They fan out, preparing to spring, their guttural hisses echoing in the uncanny stillness of this new Oregon.
"What's going on?" But I get ready for a fight anyway. There's no need to be stupid.

"What’s going on?" you growl, your voice rumbling deep in your chest, the words vibrating through the cool, heavy air. The sound of your speech ripples through the clearing like a physical force. The three creatures recoil, their yellow eyes widening, ears twitching in a frantic, rhythmic pulse. To them, your modern tongue isn't just speech; it is the resonant, crystal-clear dialect of the 'Ancient Kings'—High English. It carries a weight of authority and melodic power they haven't heard in generations, sounding more like a spell than a question.

But hunger and malice are old masters. The lead runt, a spindly thing with a scarred snout and a notched ear, shrieks a command and lunges. You move with the practiced, fluid grace of a man who has spent his life swinging steel against the stubborn hearts of Douglas firs. Your Pulaski—the heavy fire-fighting tool with its axe-blade on one side and a grub hoe on the other—becomes a lethal extension of your long, lanky arms. As the first creature leaps, you pivot on your heels, bringing the axe-head around in a brutal, horizontal arc. It catches the thing mid-air, the sharp steel biting through its leather loincloth and deep into its ribcage with a sickening, wet crunch.

Purple-black gore sprays the frost-tipped pine needles. The creature doesn't even scream; it is nearly cloven in two, dead before its broken body thuds into the dirt. You stand tall, all six-foot-three of soot-stained muscle and Swedish-Cherokee grit, the dark blood of their kin dripping from your tool. You look like a vengeful ghost of the old world descended to deliver judgment. One of the remaining runts drops its rusted blade, its bowels voiding in terror as it turns to bolt into the thick brush. The third, however, seems driven by a frantic, suicidal desperation. It drops to all fours, scurrying toward your legs with its jagged knife, aiming for your Achilles tendon.
